benchmarks.name: KernelBench
benchmarks.full_name: KernelBench: Can LLMs Write Efficient GPU Kernels?
benchmarks.notes: 250 PyTorch tasks across 4 levels (single-op / fused / full-model / HF); fast_p speedup metric. The de-facto standard (ICML'25).

benchmarks.name: robust-kbench
benchmarks.notes: Hardened KernelBench after the AI CUDA Engineer reward-hacking exploits; randomized inputs + stricter timing/correctness.

benchmarks.name: KernelBench-v3
benchmarks.notes: Community hard-variant reform of KernelBench.

benchmarks.name: KernelBenchX
benchmarks.notes: 176 tasks across a unified 15-category operator taxonomy (category-aware KernelBench).

benchmarks.name: MultiKernelBench
benchmarks.notes: 285 tasks x 14 operator categories across CUDA / Ascend C / Pallas — first cross-platform agent benchmark.

benchmarks.name: TritonBench
benchmarks.notes: 184 Triton operators (TritonBench-G real-world + TritonBench-T torch-aligned); the Triton-specific standard (ACL'25).

benchmarks.name: TritonGym
benchmarks.notes: Agentic Triton-evaluation workflow/environment.

benchmarks.name: GEAK Benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: 184 + 30 ROCm Triton tasks on MI300X; AMD Triton agent benchmark + agent.

benchmarks.name: AgentKernelArena
benchmarks.notes: 196 tasks (HIP/Triton); probes generalization to unseen configurations.

benchmarks.name: MSKernelBench
benchmarks.notes: Multi-scenario CUDA (algebra / LLM / sparse / scientific) x FP32+BF16.

benchmarks.name: CUDABench
benchmarks.notes: 1,500 prompts (500 x 3); text-to-CUDA with a roofline-based score.

benchmarks.name: NPUEval
benchmarks.notes: 102 ML operators on AMD XDNA / Ryzen AI; first NPU-kernel vectorization benchmark.

benchmarks.name: NKIBench
benchmarks.notes: Real-workload AWS Trainium NKI kernels; ships with the AccelOpt self-improving agent (MLSys'26).

benchmarks.name: NPUKernelBench
benchmarks.notes: Ascend C kernel-gen tasks; companion to AscendKernelGen / AscendKernelGen study.

benchmarks.name: CANN Bench
benchmarks.notes: CANN-framework operator benchmark for Ascend.

benchmarks.name: FlashInfer-Bench
benchmarks.notes: Real LLM-serving operators (fused MoE / sparse attention / gated delta net) on B200, using FlashInfer-Trace workloads; the serving-family counterpart to KernelBench (MLSys'26 contest).

benchmarks.name: SOL-ExecBench
benchmarks.notes: 235 problems / 124 models on Blackwell; Speed-of-Light (% of hardware peak) scoring to resist timer gaming.

benchmarks.name: ISO-Bench
benchmarks.notes: Real-world inference workload benchmark.

benchmarks.name: ComputeEval
benchmarks.notes: 566 problems (2026.1 datapack); NVIDIA CUDA-C++ suite with versioned datapacks.

benchmarks.name: BackendBench
benchmarks.notes: Evaluates generated kernels as a PyTorch backend (ATen-op correctness + perf).

benchmarks.name: KernelCraft
benchmarks.notes: Close-to-metal generation for emerging hardware.

benchmarks.name: ParEval
benchmarks.full_name: Can Large Language Models Write Parallel Code?
benchmarks.notes: 420 prompts (12 problem types x 5 x 7 execution models); a hinge case — built as a benchmark but widely reused as a substrate by later agents.

benchmarks.name: KernelBench-MUSA (MooreEval)
benchmarks.notes: MUSA-ported KernelBench + MooreEval distributed execution-feedback verifier; first domestic-GPU (Moore Threads) kernel-gen benchmark.

benchmarks.name: AscendKernelBench (AKG-AGENT)
benchmarks.notes: ~198/214-operator LLM kernel-gen evaluation platform bundled with AKG-AGENT.

benchmarks.name: PolyBench/C
benchmarks.notes: 30 polyhedral kernels (linear-algebra / solvers / datamining / stencils); POLYBENCH_DUMP_ARRAYS oracle + anti-DCE guards. Upstream = Pouchet (OSU/CSU); LLVM/Polly uses the Meinersbur mirror.

benchmarks.name: PolyBench-ACC
benchmarks.notes: ~29 PolyBench kernels in 5 accelerator models with per-kernel host-vs-device checks; the suite CUDAnalyst (ICML'26) optimizes alongside NPB + XSBench.

benchmarks.name: PolyBench/GPU
benchmarks.notes: 15 GPU kernels with built-in CPU-vs-GPU comparison; the port PolyBench-ACC grew from.

benchmarks.name: PolyBench/Python
benchmarks.notes: 30 kernels for the Python/NumPy ecosystem with cross-verification against PolyBench/C; adjacent to NPBench.

benchmarks.name: PolyBench/Fortran
benchmarks.notes: Fortran port of the 30-kernel PolyBench/C 3.2 roster; substrate for Fortran/OpenMP HPC translation.

benchmarks.name: PolyBench-NN
benchmarks.notes: DNN layers (conv/pool/RNN/LSTM, fwd+bwd) as affine PolyBench/C programs — a bridge from polyhedral to DL kernels.

benchmarks.name: PolyBench-RAJA
benchmarks.notes: PolyBench 4.2.1 via the RAJA portability layer (CPU/OpenMP/CUDA backends).

benchmarks.name: NPB (NAS Parallel Benchmarks)
benchmarks.notes: 8 kernels/pseudo-apps (BT CG EP FT IS LU MG SP) +UA/DC/DT, per-class checksum verification. GPU ports: SNU-NPB(C/OpenCL/CUDA), NPB-CPP, NPB-GPU(CUDA+HIP, GMAP), openacc-npb, NPB-Rust.

benchmarks.name: XSBench
benchmarks.notes: Monte-Carlo neutron cross-section lookup (memory-latency-bound); verification hash + FOM. The single most-used DOE proxy for LLM kernel/translation agents.

benchmarks.name: RSBench
benchmarks.notes: Multipole cross-section companion to XSBench; compute-bound contrast (on-the-fly reconstruction).

benchmarks.name: miniFE
benchmarks.notes: Implicit FE assembly + CG solve (SpMV-bound); compact proxy for sparse iterative solvers.

benchmarks.name: LULESH
benchmarks.notes: Unstructured Lagrangian shock hydro; heavy indirect addressing — a stress test for memory-access optimization. Validates final origin energy.

benchmarks.name: HPGMG
benchmarks.notes: Geometric multigrid (FV/FE); stencil + restriction/prolongation kernels, F-cycle convergence check. Top500/HPCG-complementary ranking.

benchmarks.name: CoMD
benchmarks.notes: Classical MD (LJ/EAM, cell lists); CUDA + OpenCL reference ports make a clean source/target pair for translation agents.

benchmarks.name: miniMD
benchmarks.notes: Neighbor-list MD (LAMMPS proxy); force_compute + neighbor_build are classic parallelization targets.

benchmarks.name: Quicksilver
benchmarks.notes: Branchy, divergent MC particle transport (Mercury proxy); a worst-case GPU-divergence target. CORAL-2 benchmark.

benchmarks.name: Nekbone
benchmarks.notes: Spectral-element CFD; small dense tensor-product Ax mat-vec — a canonical OpenACC/CUDA-Fortran port target. Rare Fortran substrate.

benchmarks.name: SU3_Bench
benchmarks.notes: Lattice-QCD SU(3) complex mat-mul (MILC-derived, bandwidth-bound); also mirrored in HeCBench.

benchmarks.name: BabelStream
benchmarks.notes: The reference attainable-memory-bandwidth benchmark (GPU STREAM) across 15+ programming models — an ideal Rosetta-Stone for translation/portability agents.

benchmarks.name: PENNANT
benchmarks.notes: 2D unstructured-mesh hydro (gather/scatter over polygons); CORAL-2 benchmark complementary to LULESH.

benchmarks.name: miniAMR
benchmarks.notes: Octree AMR + stencil + dynamic load-balancing; tests dynamic data structures. No in-repo CUDA — GPU agents must port it.

benchmarks.name: SimpleMOC
benchmarks.notes: Deterministic method-of-characteristics neutron transport; SimpleMOC-kernel is a repo-level translation testbed.

benchmarks.name: HeCBench
benchmarks.notes: 700+ programs (~350 distinct) each ported across 4 models with built-in verification; the dominant multi-language superset substrate for cross-model kernel agents.

benchmarks.name: Rodinia
benchmarks.notes: ~23 dwarf-guided apps (backprop, bfs, hotspot, srad, lud, nw, particlefilter); the most-cited classic heterogeneous GPU suite.

benchmarks.name: Parboil
benchmarks.notes: 11 throughput apps (sgemm, spmv, stencil, lbm, mri-q, bfs); multiple optimization levels per kernel + a correctness-checking driver.

benchmarks.name: SHOC
benchmarks.notes: 20+ benchmarks across 3 levels + stress tests; dual CUDA+OpenCL reference impls. Lineage parent of Altis/Mirovia and (multi-lang) HeCBench.

benchmarks.name: Altis
benchmarks.notes: SHOC successor for modern GPUs (unified memory, HyperQ) + DNN kernels; CXL fork altis-cxl.

benchmarks.name: Mirovia
benchmarks.notes: SHOC/Rodinia-derived suite treating DNNs as first-class; close lineage to Altis.

benchmarks.name: Hetero-Mark
benchmarks.notes: ~11 CPU-GPU collaborative benchmarks with built-in verification across CUDA/HIP/HC/OpenCL.

benchmarks.name: Chai
benchmarks.notes: 14 benchmarks x up to 7 variants exercising shared virtual memory + system-wide atomics; FPGA fork chai-fpga.

benchmarks.name: OpenDwarfs
benchmarks.notes: The most explicit 1:1 realization of all 13 Berkeley dwarfs in OpenCL — incl. the rare motifs (FSM=tdm, combinational=crc, B&B=nqueens) others skip.

benchmarks.name: CUDA Samples
benchmarks.notes: 180+ reference CUDA kernels/idioms (bandwidthTest, reductions, matmul, FFT) with run_tests.py; common seed/example source for agents.

benchmarks.name: GPGPU-Sim ISPASS-2009
benchmarks.notes: 11 historic CUDA workloads (AES, BFS, MUM, RAY, NN) for GPU-simulator validation.

benchmarks.name: Tango
benchmarks.notes: 7 library-free DNN workloads (5 CNN + 2 RNN) with trained weights + reference predictions; runnable on simulators/FPGAs.

benchmarks.name: Tartan
benchmarks.notes: Multi-GPU interconnect micro+app suite (PCIe/NVLink/IB); substrate for distributed/collective-kernel work.

benchmarks.name: Mars
benchmarks.notes: Early GPU MapReduce framework + 6 web-data-analysis apps; mostly historical.

benchmarks.name: SYCL-Bench
benchmarks.notes: 62 device-characterization + 9 runtime SYCL codes; cross-vendor portability sibling to HeCBench.

benchmarks.name: GAP Benchmark Suite
benchmarks.notes: 6 kernels (BFS SSSP PR CC BC TC) with per-kernel verifier; the standardized graph-kernel vocabulary, widely GPU-ported.

benchmarks.name: LonestarGPU / Lonestar
benchmarks.notes: ~12 irregular apps (sssp, bfs, mst, dmr); origin of the irregular-kernel task vocabulary (IrGL-generated).

benchmarks.name: Pannotia
benchmarks.notes: 6 OpenCL graph apps (bc, color, fw, mis, prk, sssp); most-reported AMD/OpenCL graph suite, HIP/gem5 re-ports.

benchmarks.name: Gunrock
benchmarks.notes: Leading high-perf GPU graph library; each primitive ships a tuned kernel + CPU --validate (Boost) oracle.

benchmarks.name: GraphBIG
benchmarks.notes: Industry-derived CPU+GPU graph suite with shared core code + HW-counter profiling.

benchmarks.name: GARDENIA
benchmarks.notes: ~10 graph + sparse-LA + ML workloads with heavily-optimized baselines (a higher bar than Rodinia/Parboil).

benchmarks.name: GBBS
benchmarks.notes: 20+ provably-efficient Ligra-based graph problems; largest CPU graph-kernel reference, superset of the GAPBS vocabulary.

benchmarks.name: Indigo3
benchmarks.notes: 7 algos x 13 styles x 15 bug types = 41,790 race/deadlock-labeled programs — a purpose-built anti-reward-hacking / correctness-robustness substrate.

benchmarks.name: cuDNN / cuBLAS
benchmarks.notes: The closed vendor reference kernels behind PyTorch eager ops; the de-facto 'beat cuDNN/cuBLAS' bar in nearly every kernel-agent eval (via torch).

benchmarks.name: DeepBench
benchmarks.notes: The original DL operator micro-benchmark (GEMM/conv/RNN/all-reduce shapes); ROCm fork ROCm/DeepBench. Its shape lists are reused everywhere.

benchmarks.name: Triton tutorial kernels
benchmarks.notes: Expert reference Triton kernels (matmul, fused-softmax, flash-attn, layernorm) each benchmarked vs PyTorch; what Triton-generating agents must reproduce/beat.

benchmarks.name: Liger-Kernel benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: 20+ production LLM-training Triton kernels (RMSNorm, RoPE, SwiGLU, FusedLinearCrossEntropy) benchmarked vs HuggingFace.

benchmarks.name: FlashAttention benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: The reference exact-attention kernel + fwd/bwd sweep scripts (FA1-FA4); the bar any generated flash-attention kernel must clear.

benchmarks.name: xFormers benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: Memory-efficient attention (CUTLASS/Flash backends) + Triton blocks with fwd/bwd benchmarks.

benchmarks.name: PyTorch operator_benchmark
benchmarks.notes: In-tree per-ATen-operator microbenchmark framework (op_bench); the canonical way to time individual ops, tracked on a CI dashboard.

benchmarks.name: DNNMark
benchmarks.notes: Config-driven per-layer DNN primitive microbenchmark (conv/pool/BN/softmax/FC/RNN) over cuDNN/cuBLAS/MIOpen.

benchmarks.name: NVBench
benchmarks.notes: Standard CUDA kernel-timing library (clock locking, axis sweeps, CSV); the harness other op suites use to measure kernels vs cuBLAS/CUTLASS.

benchmarks.name: TenSet
benchmarks.notes: 52M measured tensor-program records over 6 platforms; the canonical learned-cost-model training/eval dataset for ML-for-systems.

benchmarks.name: TVM / Ansor
benchmarks.notes: AutoTVM templates + Ansor auto-scheduler op/subgraph tuning tasks; the standard search-based compiler baseline (Ansor's programs are what TenSet measures).

benchmarks.name: Roller
benchmarks.notes: Construction-based (non-search) op kernels; a fast-compilation autotuning baseline (OSDI'22).

benchmarks.name: Welder
benchmarks.notes: Tile-graph memory-access (fusion/tiling) compiler; its fused-kernel perf/accuracy suite is an operator-fusion baseline (OSDI'23).

benchmarks.name: Hidet
benchmarks.notes: Task-mapping DL compiler (ASPLOS'23) with a compact GPU-centric autotuning space; modern tensor-core reference kernels.

benchmarks.name: AKG (Auto Kernel Generator)
benchmarks.notes: Huawei polyhedral auto-kernel generator + op test suite; AKG-AGENT adds an LLM op-gen eval platform (AscendKernelBench) for Ascend.

benchmarks.name: CUTLASS Profiler
benchmarks.notes: Vendor reference GEMM/conv kernel library + config-sweep/verify profiler; the high-perf GEMM / Speed-of-Light baseline matmul agents are scored against.

benchmarks.name: IREE benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: End-to-end MLIR/IREE model benchmarks + comparative suite vs XLA/TFLite; the MLIR-codegen baseline.

benchmarks.name: MLIR microkernels
benchmarks.notes: Linalg/Affine codegen integration-test microkernels; the infra beneath IREE and AKG-MLIR.

benchmarks.name: SuiteSparse Matrix Collection
benchmarks.notes: ~2900+ real-world sparse matrices; the canonical input corpus behind almost every SpMV/SpMM/SpGEMM benchmark.

benchmarks.name: DLMC (Deep Learning Matrix Collection)
benchmarks.notes: ~3700 sparse matrices from pruned DNN weights (50-98% sparsity); the DL-sparsity counterpart to SuiteSparse and default SpMM/SDDMM input set.

benchmarks.name: Sputnik
benchmarks.notes: Reference sparse SpMM/SDDMM GPU kernels for DL sparsity; introduced the 1-D tiling scheme that later sparse kernels build on.

benchmarks.name: TACO benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: Tensor Algebra Compiler generating sparse SpMV/SpMM/SpGEMM/MTTKRP kernels vs Eigen/MKL/SPLATT; seeded SparseTIR.

benchmarks.name: SparseTIR
benchmarks.notes: Composable sparse-compilation IR on TensorIR (ASPLOS'23); a frequently-cited target for automating sparse-kernel scheduling.

benchmarks.name: cuSPARSE
benchmarks.notes: Vendor sparse BLAS (SpMV/SpMM/SpGEMM); the baseline sparse-kernel agents are measured against.

benchmarks.name: cuSPARSELt
benchmarks.notes: Structured (2:4) sparse tensor-core matmul library; the baseline for structured-sparsity kernels.

benchmarks.name: ASpT
benchmarks.notes: Adaptive Sparse Tiling SpMM/SDDMM kernels; a strong sparse-tiling baseline (PPoPP'19).

benchmarks.name: GE-SpMM / dgSPARSE
benchmarks.notes: General-purpose SpMM for GNNs (SC'20); part of the dgSPARSE library of sparse GPU kernels.

benchmarks.name: Magicube
benchmarks.notes: Low-precision (int8/int4) sparse SpMM/SDDMM on tensor cores for quantized sparse Transformers (SC'22).

benchmarks.name: VENOM / Spatha
benchmarks.notes: V:N:M format + Spatha SpMM enabling arbitrary N:M sparsity on tensor cores (SC'23).

benchmarks.name: vectorSparse
benchmarks.notes: Vector-wise sparse tensor-core SpMM for attention (column-vector sparsity); evaluated on DLMC.

benchmarks.name: ExaStencils / ExaSlang
benchmarks.notes: Stencil/multigrid DSL + code generator (ExaSlang → CUDA/OpenMP/MPI); a stencil-codegen reference.

benchmarks.name: Pochoir
benchmarks.notes: Cache-oblivious stencil compiler (SPAA'11); classic stencil-DSL baseline.

benchmarks.name: Devito benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: Symbolic finite-difference stencil DSL (seismic imaging); generates optimized C/OpenMP/OpenACC stencil kernels.

benchmarks.name: StencilGen (Artemis)
benchmarks.notes: High-order GPU stencil code generator with associative reordering / streaming (SC'18); strong stencil baseline.

benchmarks.name: AN5D
benchmarks.notes: Automatic stencil framework with deep temporal blocking (CGO'20).

benchmarks.name: Stencil Probe
benchmarks.notes: Lightweight stencil microbenchmark probe; one of the earliest stencil performance studies.

benchmarks.name: PolyMage
benchmarks.notes: Image-processing-pipeline DSL with automatic fusion/tiling (ASPLOS'15); a Halide-adjacent stencil/pipeline benchmark.

benchmarks.name: Halide benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: Schedule-separated image/array-processing DSL; its apps/ benchmarks (blur, camera pipe, etc.) are canonical autoscheduler targets.

benchmarks.name: heFFTe
benchmarks.notes: Highly-parallel multi-GPU FFT (ECP); the distributed-FFT reference (spectral motif).

benchmarks.name: benchFFT (FFTW)
benchmarks.notes: The canonical FFT benchmark harness comparing FFT libraries; spectral-motif reference.

benchmarks.name: HPL (High Performance Linpack)
benchmarks.notes: The TOP500 FP64 FLOPS metric (dense LU); the canonical compute roof in roofline analysis.

benchmarks.name: HPCG
benchmarks.notes: Memory-bound MG-preconditioned CG (SpMV + SymGS); the TOP500 low-locality roof complementing HPL.

benchmarks.name: HPL-MxP (HPL-AI)
benchmarks.notes: Mixed-precision LU + FP64 iterative refinement; rewards tensor-core low-precision GEMM with accuracy guarantees — the exact precision/accuracy tradeoff tensor-core kernel agents navigate.

benchmarks.name: STREAM
benchmarks.notes: The reference memory-bandwidth microbenchmark (Copy/Scale/Add/Triad); Triad is THE memory roof in every roofline model. GPU descendant = BabelStream.

benchmarks.name: mixbench
benchmarks.notes: Sweeps operational intensity to empirically trace the GPU roofline (compute roof, memory roof, ridge point) — the practical tool for roofline-aware agents.

benchmarks.name: gpumembench
benchmarks.notes: On-chip GPU memory bandwidth (L1/L2/shared/constant); the per-level roofs above DRAM for cache-blocking-aware agents.

benchmarks.name: nvbandwidth
benchmarks.notes: Official H2D/D2H/P2P/NVLink bandwidth + latency tool (replaces cuda-samples bandwidthTest); the data-movement roof for transfer-bound and multi-GPU kernels.

benchmarks.name: Empirical Roofline Toolkit (ERT)
benchmarks.notes: Auto-generates the full empirical roofline (peak GFLOP/s + bandwidth per memory level); the device characterization Speed-of-Light kernel agents depend on.

benchmarks.name: gpu-burn
benchmarks.notes: Sustained GEMM stress test (peak-FLOPS + reliability via per-iter result compare); a hardware-sanity oracle for benchmarking harnesses.

benchmarks.name: HPCC (HPC Challenge)
benchmarks.notes: Seven-in-one suite spanning the locality space (HPL, DGEMM, STREAM, PTRANS, RandomAccess/GUPS, FFT, b_eff) — all four roofline corners in one harness.

benchmarks.name: clpeak
benchmarks.notes: Synthetic OpenCL peak-capability profiler (compute/bandwidth/latency); a cross-vendor roofline-ceiling reference.

benchmarks.name: LLVM test-suite
benchmarks.notes: The LLVM correctness+performance regression suite (bundles PolyBench/Polly); a codegen substrate for compiler-side kernel experiments.

benchmarks.name: SPEC ACCEL
benchmarks.notes: Accelerator apps keyed by offload model (derived from Parboil/Rodinia/NPB/SPEC-OMP); programming-model-as-primary-axis. Cautionary tale — silently retired.

benchmarks.name: SPEC OMP2012
benchmarks.notes: 14 OpenMP application benchmarks with an energy metric; CPU-parallel codegen substrate.

benchmarks.name: MachSuite
benchmarks.notes: 12 accelerator/HLS kernels (aes, fft, gemm, spmv, stencil, bfs, viterbi, md); standard HLS/accelerator-design benchmark.

benchmarks.name: CHStone
benchmarks.notes: 12 C programs (crypto, codec, processor) — the classic C-to-HLS synthesis benchmark.

benchmarks.name: Rosetta (HLS)
benchmarks.notes: Realistic FPGA HLS benchmark suite (face detection, optical flow, BNN); performance-portability HLS reference.

benchmarks.name: HLS-Eval
benchmarks.notes: LLM-for-HLS evaluation framework — extends the kernel-agent paradigm to FPGA high-level synthesis.

benchmarks.name: C2HLSC
benchmarks.notes: LLM-driven C-to-HLS-C conversion benchmark; the HLS analog of kernel translation agents.

benchmarks.name: MLPerf Training
benchmarks.notes: Industry-standard end-to-end training benchmark (ResNet/BERT/LLM/DLRM/SD); bounds end-to-end targets, not op-level — Closed/Open division is a reusable fairness pattern.

benchmarks.name: MLPerf Inference
benchmarks.notes: Datacenter/Edge inference with SingleStream/Server/Offline scenarios; defines serving-scenario load patterns.

benchmarks.name: MLPerf Tiny
benchmarks.notes: Microcontroller-tier ML (KWS, VWW, anomaly detection); the embedded end of the deployment-scale axis.

benchmarks.name: AIBench
benchmarks.notes: Scenario/component/micro AI benchmark family (with HPC AI500, Edge AIBench, AIoTBench); spans datacenter to IoT.

benchmarks.name: DAWNBench
benchmarks.notes: Pioneered time-to-accuracy / cost-to-train metrics; folded into MLPerf.

benchmarks.name: AI-Benchmark
benchmarks.notes: On-device (mobile/desktop) DL inference+training benchmark across NN ops; hardware-comparison oriented.

benchmarks.name: Fathom
benchmarks.notes: 8 archetypal DL workloads chosen for representativeness/diversity; model-level profiling substrate.

benchmarks.name: TorchBench
benchmarks.notes: ~100 model workloads + operator userbenchmarks; one leg of the TorchInductor triad surfacing PyTorch perf regressions.

benchmarks.name: TorchInductor / Dynamo suite
benchmarks.notes: The torchbench/HuggingFace/timm triad + Performance Dashboard; TorchInductor max-autotune Triton output is the strongest automated baseline agents must beat.

benchmarks.name: NVIDIA Transformer Engine benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: FP8/FP4 GEMM + fused transformer-block (LayerNormMLP, attention) reference; the optimization ceiling for low-precision transformer kernel agents.

benchmarks.name: Megatron-LM benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: Large-scale training throughput (MFU/HFU) across TP/PP/DP/EP; defines GEMM + comm-overlap shapes at scale (system-level ceiling).

benchmarks.name: vLLM benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: De-facto serving benchmark (throughput, TTFT, TPOT, ITL); defines the end-to-end metric attention/MoE/sampling kernel improvements are judged against.

benchmarks.name: SGLang benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: bench_one_batch is a kernel-level prefill/decode latency profiler (bypasses scheduler) — the most kernel-relevant serving harness; source of Astra's production kernels.

benchmarks.name: LLMPerf
benchmarks.notes: Black-box endpoint load tester (ITL, throughput, TTFT) + public leaderboard; service-level, defines the throughput/TTFT vocabulary.

benchmarks.name: GenAI-Perf
benchmarks.notes: NVIDIA serving benchmark across NIM/Triton/TensorRT-LLM (TTFT/ITL/goodput); OpenAI-API compatible. Superseded by AIPerf.

benchmarks.name: AIPerf
benchmarks.notes: Active GenAI-Perf replacement; same OpenAI-API serving target (TTFT/ITL/TPS/RPS).

benchmarks.name: FlexAttention / attention-gym
benchmarks.notes: Attention-variant microbenchmark (causal/sliding-window/ALiBi/document mask via score_mod) AND a kernel-generation model; measured vs FA2/FA3/SDPA.

benchmarks.name: Graphcore IPU benchmarks
benchmarks.notes: IPU substrate: examples-utils benchmarks, BabelStream IPU port, flash-attention-ipu Poplar kernel. No known LLM kernel-agent targets IPU yet.

benchmarks.name: Tenstorrent tt-metal
benchmarks.notes: Tensix kernel substrate: TT-NN operator library + TT-Metalium low-level kernels (reader/compute/writer). Growing low-level programmability; no published kernel-agent benchmark yet.

benchmarks.name: Cambricon mlu-ops
benchmarks.notes: Official Cambricon BANG C operator library; the realistic domestic-NPU substrate a kernel agent targets (cf. QiMeng-Xpiler CUDA→BANG).

benchmarks.name: Ascend cann-ops
benchmarks.notes: Huawei's official Ascend C operator library; the 127-operator baseline corpus AscendOptimizer optimizes against (+ cann-ops-adv fusion lib).

benchmarks.name: QiMeng-Xpiler
benchmarks.notes: Neural-symbolic tensor-program transcompiler benchmark (21 ops x 8 shapes = 168 cases across 4 DLS); flags CUDA→BANG as the hardest direction (OSDI'25).

benchmarks.name: QiMeng-TensorOp
benchmarks.notes: One-line-prompt tensor-operator generation with hardware primitives via LLM+MCTS; emphasizes RISC-V (domestic ISA), beats OpenBLAS/cuBLAS baselines (IJCAI'25).

kernelbench.use_when: You need comparability with the de-facto standard everyone reports — never as sole evidence.

robust-kbench.use_when: You want to prove your speedups survive anti-gaming filters (the KernelBench exploit fixes).

backendbench.baseline: PyTorch op semantics (correctness-first; kernels run as a pip backend)
backendbench.use_when: You want production-shape correctness and a path to actually shipping kernels as a backend.

sol-execbench.baseline: agent-optimized PyTorch baseline + analytical speed-of-light ceiling (datasheet peak)
sol-execbench.use_when: You want an absolute (ceiling-relative) score on production LLM kernels — datasheet-ceiling caveat applies.

cudabench.use_when: You want a roofline-relative Performance-Score with profiler-measured intensity (datasheet-ceiling caveat).

iso-bench.use_when: You want real serving-stack tasks (vLLM/SGLang merged PRs) with did-it-fix-the-bottleneck attribution.

geak.use_when: You target ROCm/Triton and want budget-decomposed (sequential vs parallel) reporting.

tritonbench.baseline: PyTorch/reference impl; descriptive GPU-efficiency vs A100 theoretical peak
tritonbench.use_when: You generate Triton specifically and want real-world (GitHub-sourced) operators.

multikernelbench.use_when: You need multi-platform coverage (CUDA / Ascend C / TPU Pallas) and accept per-platform-only numbers.

computeeval.use_when: You want CUDA correctness with anti-overfitting (hidden tests) from NVIDIA's own harness.

kernelbench-v3.use_when: You want a community-hardened KernelBench variant reported across several GPUs.
